---
format: pdf
linestretch: 1.5
fontsize: 12pt
mainfont: Times New Roman
geometry:
      - paper=a4paper
      - inner=2.5cm
      - outer=3.8cm
      - top=2cm
      - bottom=3cm
number-sections: true
author: Tobias Zeier

bibliography: ../references.bib
csl: ../harvard-cite-them-right-no-et-al.csl
---

# Literature Review

This chapter develops the theoretical and empirical foundation for the proposed framework through a critical review of four interconnected bodies of literature: the conceptual foundations of technical debt, existing measurement and prioritisation approaches, technical debt governance in financial and regulated enterprise IT, and multi-criteria decision-making. Each section synthesises convergent findings, identifies tensions and limitations, and positions these gaps in relation to the research design. The review adopts a critical, problem-oriented perspective rather than an exhaustive catalogue, building a coherent argument that existing approaches are not adequate for service-level enterprise IT contexts and that a quantitative framework applying TOPSIS to operational service management data represents a defensible and novel contribution.

## Context of the Topic

Technical debt has become both a central concept and a persistent management problem in software engineering and enterprise IT governance 
[@avgeriouTechnicalDebtManagement2023]. Ward Cunningham first introduced the metaphor in 1992, describing technical debt as the long-term cost of short-term technical compromises [@cunninghamWyCashPortfolioManagement1992]. Over time, the concept has expanded from a focus on poor code to a broader understanding that also includes architectural, infrastructure, organisational and process aspects of complex IT systems [@kruchtenTechnicalDebtMetaphor2012].

Despite this conceptual expansion, much of the empirical and tool-supported literature remains anchored at the source code level, relying heavily on static analysis tools and code-based metrics. These approaches work tolerably well for greenfield development dominated by in-house code but are much less appropriate for enterprise environments where operational reliability depends on vendor platforms, legacy infrastructure and tightly integrated service stacks [@amanatidisEvaluatingAgreementTechnical2020].

The present review is motivated by a gap at the intersection of technical debt management, multi-criteria decision-making and mission-critical enterprise IT operations. To the best of current literature, no framework provides a quantitative, service-level mechanism for measuring and prioritising technical debt in environments such as trading IT. In these contexts, the most consequential forms of debt manifest not in code repositories but in operational outcomes such as incident frequency, recovery time, patch delay and change failure rate [@ramasubbuControllingTechnicalDebt2021]. The following sections build a theoretical and methodological basis for the proposed framework.

## Methodology for Literature Selection

The literature search was conducted between January and March 2026 using a systematic and reproducible approach, drawing on four principal academic databases: the University of Essex library, Scopus, IEEE Xplore and Google Scholar. Search tearms included the following.

| Theme | Search String |
|---|---|
| TD foundations | `"technical debt" AND (metaphor OR taxonomy OR typology OR theory)` |
| TD measurement and tools | `"technical debt" AND (measurement OR "static analysis" OR SonarQube OR metric OR "code smell")` |
| TD prioritisation | `"technical debt" AND (priorit* OR "backlog management" OR remediation OR repayment)` |
| TD in enterprise and financial IT | `"technical debt" AND ("enterprise" OR "financial services" OR "trading" OR "legacy system" OR "regulated")` |
| TD automation | `"technical debt management" AND ("machine learning" OR intelligent OR automation OR NLP)` |
| MCDM and TOPSIS | `TOPSIS AND ("multi-criteria" OR MCDM OR "SAW" OR "decision making" OR priorit*)` |
| Patch management | `"patch management" AND (priorit* OR risk OR vulnerability OR SLA OR enterprise)` |
| Operational risk and DORA | `("DORA metrics" OR "mean time to restore") AND ("technical debt" OR "software delivery")` |
: Search terms used for literature review {#tbl-status tbl-colwidths="[30,70]"}

Sources were screened against the following pre-specified inclusion and exclusion criteria:

| Criterion | Rule |
|---|---|
| Publication type | Peer-reviewed journal articles, conference proceedings or empirical technical reports |
| Language | English full text available |
| Date range | 2019–2026, or foundational regardless of date |
| Thematic relevance | Directly addresses at least one of the four thematic pillars |
| Exclusion: scope | Opinion pieces without empirical grounding |
| Exclusion: topic | Agile process improvement without a measurable TD component |
| Exclusion: duplicates | Superseded by a more comprehensive journal version |
: Inclusion and exclusion criteria {#tbl-criteria tbl-colwidths="[30,70]"}

Screening was conducted in two stages. Titles and abstracts of all retrieved records were first assessed against these criteria, reducing the initial pool. Remaining sources then underwent full-text review to verify relevance and methodological quality. Forward and backward citation snowballing was subsequently applied to three anchor publications: @kruchtenTechnicalDebtMetaphor2012, @lenarduzziSystematicLiteratureReview2021 and @forsgrenAccelerate2018 to surface relevant studies not captured by database searches. The process yielded approximately 140 candidate sources, of which more than 60 were retained following full-text screening.


The review uses narrative synthesis rather than a formal systematic review protocol. The research spans three disciplines, namely software engineering, enterprise IT governance and decision science, which differ significantly in their research methods, terminology and evidence standards. A single meta-analytic framework would not accommodate this heterogeneity, and the aim of the review is to build a coherent argument in support of a novel design science artefact, for which narrative synthesis is the appropriate approach [@hevnerDesignScienceInformation2004]. The following four sections build the theoretical and contextual case for the proposed framework in sequence.

## Conceptual Foundations of Technical Debt

The first thematic area traces the evolution of the technical debt concept from the original metaphor to later theoretical developments, and then to its
expansion beyond software into systems engineering, infrastructure and vendor platforms. Understanding this evolution is essential because most existing tools and practices are still tuned to an earlier, narrower view of technical debt that misaligns with the realities of contemporary enterprise IT.

### Origins and the Metaphor

@cunninghamWyCashPortfolioManagement1992 introduced the metaphor to explain his refactoring strategy to non-technical stakeholders: shipping "not quite right" code resembles taking on financial debt, with each subsequent maintenance effort representing interest. If the principal is not repaid through refactoring, the cost of working with the system compounds until change becomes very difficult.

The debt metaphor has proved productive, but it carries important conceptual limits. In financial debt, the principal is contractually defined, and the interest rate is known in advance, whereas technical debt has neither. The principal is an estimated remediation cost that changes as systems evolve. The interest depends on future development activity that is uncertain at the time of incurrence. These limitations matter directly for the present research because a central objective of the proposed framework is to make interest cost concrete and operationally measurable. The framework therefore does not rely on the metaphor for rigour; it grounds its estimates in service-level operational data rather than in abstract financial analogies.

Several influential practitioners refined the concept. @mcconnellManagingTechnicalDebt2008 introduced a cost structure distinguishing principal, recurring interest and compounding interest, and differentiated unintentional debt from intentional debt incurred as a conscious trade-off. @fowlerTechnicalDebtQuadrant2009 proposed a quadrant distinguishing reckless from prudent debt and deliberate from inadvertent debt. It should be noted that the quadrant originates in a practitioner blog post rather than a peer-reviewed publication; it is a useful heuristic for stakeholder communication but should not be treated as a theoretically validated framework. @kruchtenTechnicalDebtMetaphor2012 subsequently formalised a taxonomy covering code, design, architectural, test, build and documentation debt on the basis of a structured research programme, and this taxonomy underpins much of the subsequent empirical work.

### Theoretical Development

Empirical studies have confirmed and extended this view. @beskerTechnicalDebtEmpirical2020 reported that developers in industrial settings spend roughly 23% of their time dealing with the consequences of existing technical debt, with architectural debt exerting the strongest negative influence on productivity. @bediUnderstandingFactorsAffecting2022 used regression analysis on open-source repositories and found that commit frequency, lines of code, code smells, and test coverage are significant predictors of technical debt, whereas developer reputation metrics are not. This suggests that technical debt is driven primarily by development practices rather than by the perceived quality of individual contributors.

@ahmadTechnicalDebtNot2026 approached the topic qualitatively, interviewing experts from four international companies. They identified five non-technical dimensions of debt accumulation in large-scale agile environments, namely social dynamics, process, people, documentation and requirements, with lack of communication emerging as the dominant driver. This reinforces the view of technical debt as a sociotechnical phenomenon that cannot be governed by technical measurement alone.

@vidoniInfiniteTechnicalDebt2022 advanced a more radical argument through a game-theoretic lens, proposing that in complex systems technical debt cannot be completely removed and that its effects cannot be fully controlled even when individual items are repaid. The concept of "infinite technical debt" challenges the assumption, implicit in many repayment-focused frameworks, that debt is always recoverable given sufficient effort. This argument has not yet been empirically validated in enterprise IT settings and should be understood as a theoretical provocation rather than an established finding. Its practical implication for the present framework is that governance outputs should surface replacement decisions where service-level data suggests debt has crossed a threshold beyond which incremental repayment is unlikely to be effective.

### Expanding the Conceptual Perimeter

The conceptual perimeter of technical debt has progressively widened to include broader systems and lifecycle concerns. @kleinwaksTechnicalDebtSystems2023 conducted a systematic review of technical debt in systems engineering and found that requirements, architecture, integration artefacts and verification artefacts can all accumulate debt, and that many software-oriented approaches are poorly adapted to multidisciplinary systems engineering lifecycles. @hanssenIdentifyingScalabilityDebt2019 identified scalability debt as a distinct subtype triggered by regulatory change: in an open banking case study, they showed that architectural decisions, adequate before the EU Payment Services Directive 2 (PSD2), became sources of debt once new scalability requirements were imposed without architectural review. This illustrates how external regulatory change can convert previously adequate designs into liabilities, a pattern directly relevant to financial trading IT where regulatory obligations are similarly dynamic. @yangTaxonomyTechnicalDebt2019 proposed a taxonomy of Commercial Off-the-Shelf (COTS) related technical debt covering seven key types, including vendor platform obsolescence and integration constraints. Managing such forms of debt requires different tools from those used for internally developed code. For financial trading IT, where vendor platforms and COTS products are central, this broader view is particularly important: many of the most consequential debts, including platform debt, infrastructure debt and vulnerability debt, are not detectable by standard static analysis.

## Measurement and Prioritisation Frameworks

The second thematic area examines how technical debt is measured and prioritised in practice. It argues that current measurement is heavily concentrated at the code level and that existing prioritisation frameworks lack the multidimensional quantitative rigour required for robust governance in enterprise settings.

### The Dominance of Static Analysis and Its Limits

Static code analysis tools such as SonarQube, SQALE and CAST dominate current practice, typically operationalising technical debt as a weighted sum of estimated remediation effort derived from rule violations [@biazottoTechnicalDebtManagement2024]. In a systematic mapping study, @biazottoTechnicalDebtManagement2024 identified 178 primary studies and found that most automation artefacts operate at the code level, that substantial human intervention is still required even at higher automation levels, and that end-to-end automation across the technical debt management lifecycle is rare. The focus on code leaves infrastructure-level and platform-level debt largely unaddressed. @ampatzoglouSDK4EDPlatformTechnical2022 presented SDK4ED as a comprehensive industry oriented platform integrating several code level techniques. A three-year evaluation suggested that SDK4ED can deliver financial benefits to software organisations. At the same time, the platform offers only limited support for debt related to infrastructure or vendor platforms. @tornhillCodeRedBusiness2022 analysed 39 proprietary production codebases and found that poor code quality is statistically associated with higher defect density, longer remediation cycles and reduced developer throughput, confirming that code-level debt has clear business consequences. This does not change the fact that an exclusive focus on code creates blind spots: organisations may allocate significant resources to refactoring while leaving critical legacy infrastructure and unsupported components unaddressed.

@amanatidisEvaluatingAgreementTechnical2020 evaluated agreement between different technical debt assessment tools applied to 50 open-source projects and observed substantial divergence: tools applied to the same codebase produce different estimates and highlight different violation sets, with no single tool consistently dominating. This implies that a debt figure generated by any one tool is a function of that tool's ruleset and thresholds rather than an objective measurement. @albuquerqueManagingTechnicalDebt2023 reinforced this finding at scale, noting that infrastructure debt and process debt remain significantly understudied compared to code and architectural debt.

### The Prioritisation Gap

@lenarduzziSystematicLiteratureReview2021 carried out the most comprehensive review of technical debt prioritisation strategies to date, analysing 44 primary studies. They found that existing approaches cluster around cost-based, value-based and risk-based strategies, that most adopt a single primary criterion, that quantitative methods are less common than heuristic techniques, and that empirical validation on industrial datasets is rare. They concluded that the field lacks agreement on which factors matter, how to measure them and what constitutes an adequate prioritisation process. This conclusion directly motivates the quantitative contribution of the present work. The stakes of inadequate prioritisation are concrete: @detoledoReducingIncidentsMicroservices2021 showed in a production microservices system that targeted repayment of architectural technical debt led to an 84% reduction in incidents, anchoring the case for prioritisation in a measurable operational outcome.

Empirical studies consistently reveal a gap between tool-generated priorities and practitioner behaviour. Practitioners consider both value and cost rather than a single strategy, and perceived value is highly context-dependent [@alfayezHowSonarQubeidentifiedTechnical2023]. @pinaTechnicalDebtPrioritization2022 surveyed developers and found that prioritisation decisions reflect a combination of technical severity, business impact and available effort, and that developers frequently experience tension between technically optimal repayment choices and organisationally imposed constraints. Most IT managers nonetheless lack a formal technical debt management process, with cross-functional communication identified as the primary obstacle [@wieseITManagersPerspective2023]. @stochelBusinessdrivenTechnicalDebt2023 proposed the Continuous Debt Valuation Approach (CoDVA), which assigns a financial value to each debt item based on remediation cost, interest rate and business risk exposure; case studies show that financial valuation can significantly change repayment priorities relative to effort-only approaches, but CoDVA still relies on development-level data and does not incorporate operational service management signals such as incident
rates or change failure rates.

These studies collectively indicate that any quantitative prioritisation framework embedding value judgements about which systems and risks matter most, must make those judgements transparent and auditable. Particularly in regulated institutions, where priority scores may influence funding, staffing and post-incident accountability.

## Technical Debt in Financial and Regulated Enterprise IT

The third thematic area applies these general insights to the specific setting of financial services and regulated trading IT, focusing on how debt accumulates in these environments, how it translates into operational harm, and how related work in security and patch management quantifies that harm.

### The Financial Services Context

Financial services organisations are characterised by complex, layered legacies, strict regulatory compliance requirements and stringent service availability obligations, with architectures that often depend on vendor platforms and COTS products that cannot be freely refactored [@hakiDigitalNudgingTechnical2023].

@hakiDigitalNudgingTechnical2023 conducted clinical research at Credit Suisse, designing and introducing a digital nudge for technical debt management across more than 3,000 IT applications. As part of this work, they defined a technical debt taxonomy with four main categories: code quality debt, infrastructure lifecycle debt, vulnerability debt and automation debt. Infrastructure lifecycle debt was measured as cumulative months of unsupported components; vulnerability debt was derived from weighted counts of scanner-detected vulnerabilities. This taxonomy is significant because it demonstrates that, at scale, a major financial institution assigns at least as much operational importance to infrastructure and vulnerability debt as to code-level debt, directly supporting the broader measurement approach taken in the present research.

The empirical basis of the evaluation is, however, limited. The quantitative analysis focused on three development teams, two of which reduced their debt index over one year while the third did not. The authors identified management intent as the decisive factor, rather than the nudge design itself. The clinical research design, with researchers embedded in the organisation, also raises questions about independence and replicability. These factors do not invalidate the conceptual contribution, but they caution against generalising from the observed outcomes to other institutions or debt types.

@rollandExploringTensionsManagement2021 investigated the organisational dynamics of architectural debt accumulation at a large Scandinavian financial institution through eleven in-depth interviews with business managers, enterprise architects and developers. Their analysis identified two persistent tensions: between decentralised feature delivery teams optimising for local throughput and the governance required to manage debt at the system level, and between the pace of digital innovation and the long-term sustainability of the supporting architecture. Both tensions are intensified in financial services by compliance obligations and competitive pressure to release new products rapidly. The study is notable for grounding these dynamics in empirical data from a regulated banking institution. Its primary limitation is the single-case design; whether the patterns observed generalise to institutions with different architectural profiles, including trading-specific environments, remains an open question.

### Enterprise Systems and the Outsourcing Context

@bankerTechnicalDebtFirm2020 examined 26 firms operating a commercial COTS CRM system over its full eleven-year lifecycle. Using propensity score matching against firms with zero technical debt, they found that a 10% increase in technical debt reduced gross return on assets by 16% on average. Critically, this penalty more than doubled within six years of adoption as code decay compounded structural complexity. While all firms initially benefited from system customisation, those carrying median debt levels saw the net value of their system turn negative by year five, a pattern robust to alternative performance measures including asset turnover, gross margin and operating income.

In a later study, @ramasubbuControllingTechnicalDebt2021 extended this analysis to 1,824 outsourced remediation projects involving 424 client organisations. They found that "control balancing" between client and vendor improved remediation outcomes only when both parties had agreed a long-term migration roadmap. In the absence of such a roadmap, increased coordination actually harmed performance. For prioritisation frameworks, this implies that ranking debt items without embedding them in a strategic roadmap can be counterproductive.

@rinta-kahilaGettingTrappedTechnical2023 examined a global manufacturing firm's COTS implementation and showed how staged legacy replacement, combined with recurrent customisations to restore local flexibility, led to self-reinforcing architectural debt accumulation. A system dynamics model captured reinforcing feedback loops between customisation decisions, debt accumulation and declining digital options. This work suggests that organisations do not typically neglect technical debt deliberately. Instead, they are trapped by interactions between technical design choices and organisational routines that standard code-level tools cannot reveal. Governance-level visibility of infrastructure and architectural debt is required to detect these dynamics before they become irreversible.

### DORA Metrics as Operational Debt Proxies

@forsgrenAccelerate2018 developed and validated the DORA metrics across more than 2,000 organisations, showing that deployment frequency, lead time for changes, mean time to restore service and change failure rate distinguish high-performing from low-performing delivery teams. A crucial feature of these metrics is that they can be derived from operational systems such as CI/CD pipelines and ITSM tools without access to source code.

It is important to acknowledge that the DORA metrics were validated as predictors of software delivery performance, not as proxies for technical debt severity. The inferential step from degraded DORA indicators to elevated technical debt is plausible but not yet empirically established as a general relationship in the technical debt literature. The present framework therefore formalises the following as a core research hypothesis: that degraded DORA-derived operational indicators are valid proxies for technical debt severity in vendor-dominated enterprise IT environments. Rather than borrowing this relationship as an established premise from prior work, the present study treats it as testable. The empirical evaluation phase will assess it through correlation analysis between indicator scores and historical incident and remediation records, and by testing ranking stability when criterion weights are varied.

@paudelExploringRelationshipTechnical2025 investigated the relationship between technical debt density and lead time across six components at a large fintech company. The results were mixed: two components showed a moderate positive correlation, two a moderate negative correlation and two no meaningful relationship. Technical debt density explained between 5% and 41% of lead time variance depending on the component. The authors concluded that technical debt alone cannot explain lead time and that change size, component ownership and team structure are likely confounding factors. These mixed results reinforce the case for multi-criteria measurement and indicate that DORA-derived proxies must be used as one input among several rather than as standalone indicators.

The following table maps each operational indicator used in the proposed framework to the technical debt dimension it proxies and the literature that justifies its inclusion:

| Indicator | TD Dimension | Literature Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Incident frequency | Architectural debt | @detoledoReducingIncidentsMicroservices2021; @ramasubbuControllingTechnicalDebt2021 |
| Mean Time to Restore (MTTR) | Infrastructure / platform debt | @forsgrenAccelerate2018; @paudelExploringRelationshipTechnical2025 |
| Change failure rate | Process debt | @forsgrenAccelerate2018; @paudelExploringRelationshipTechnical2025 |
| Patch lag | Vulnerability debt | @tizioSoftwareUpdatesStrategies2023; @markkanenPatchManagementPlanning2023 |
| Unsupported component months | Infrastructure lifecycle debt | @hakiDigitalNudgingTechnical2023 |
: Mapping of operational indicators to technical debt dimensions and literature justification {#tbl-indicators tbl-colwidths="[25,30,45]"}

Using operational service management data as a basis for technical debt prioritisation raises important ethical questions. Such records are generated and owned by operations teams, and development teams may not have consented to their use in performance evaluations. These considerations are addressed in the methodology chapter.

### Security Debt and Patch Management

Security-related technical debt is most visible in the literature on patch management and vulnerability handling. @tizioSoftwareUpdatesStrategies2023 analysed 86 Advanced Persistent Threat campaigns dated between 2008 and 2020, finding that enterprises delaying updates by one month faced 4.9 times higher odds of compromise compared to immediate patching, rising to 9.1 times at three months. Contrary to common assumption, most campaigns exploited publicly known vulnerabilities rather than zero-days, confirming that unpatched systems carry measurable, quantifiable risk. These figures should be contextualised carefully: the dataset spans all industries and predates regulatory changes, including FINMA 2023/1 and the EU DORA regulation, that have since accelerated patching cycles in financial services specifically [@finmaCircular202312024]. The risk multipliers are therefore best treated as indicative of the direction and order of magnitude of the relationship rather than as directly applicable benchmarks for contemporary trading IT.

@markkanenPatchManagementPlanning2023 reviewed current NIST guidance and the academic literature on patch management planning. They argue that uniform, time-based patching policies are increasingly inadequate given the shrinking window between vulnerability disclosure and exploitation, and that organisations should treat patching as a risk-based, organisation-wide business priority rather than a purely technical responsibility. @nursePatchNotPatch2025 framed patch prioritisation as a problem of maintaining SLA compliance, noting that business partners and clients drive patching decisions through supply chain agreements and contractual requirements. This aligns patch management directly with the service-level focus of the present research.

@kulaReleasingFastSlow2019 studied release practices and dependency management at ING, showing that keeping upstream dependencies current is a persistent challenge in rapid-release banking environments and that delays in dependency updates contribute directly to technical debt. This finding is consistent with the broader argument, that technical debt manifests in ways detectable through operational service management data. The study is based on a single-organisation case study, however, and may reflect ING-specific governance practices; whether the same dependency dynamics apply in trading IT environments with different release governance structures is not established.

Together, these studies confirm that the most consequential forms of technical debt in financial IT are operational rather than code-level, establishing the need for a measurement approach grounded in service management data.

## Multi-Criteria Decision-Making and TOPSIS

The fourth thematic area argues that multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) methods are well suited to the technical debt prioritisation problem and that TOPSIS is more appropriate for this context than simpler alternatives.

### The Case for Multi-Criteria Methods

Technical debt prioritisation involves balancing several criteria simultaneously: remediation cost, business value, security risk, operational impact and regulatory compliance. Practitioners weigh these dimensions together rather than applying a single factor [@alfayezHowSonarQubeidentifiedTechnical2023]. @lenarduzziSystematicLiteratureReview2021 concluded that the absence of a validated, quantitative, multi-criteria framework is a central obstacle to evidence-based technical debt governance.

A weighted scoring matrix is the simplest alternative, but it assumes that criteria are independent and commensurable across scales, neither of which holds reliably when combining continuous cost metrics with ordinal or binary compliance indicators. A risk matrix is categorical and does not support fine-grained ranking of a large service portfolio. TOPSIS addresses both limitations by normalising heterogeneous criteria before aggregation and ranking alternatives by their geometric proximity to the ideal solution, making it well suited to contexts where criteria differ in type, scale and direction of preference [@ciardielloComparisonTOPSISSAW2023].

### TOPSIS: Mechanics, Applications and Critical Limitations

@hwangMultipleAttributeDecision1981 introduced TOPSIS, defining the ideal solution as the hypothetical alternative scoring best on all criteria simultaneously and ranking alternatives by Euclidean proximity to this ideal. @zanakisMultiattributeDecisionMaking1998 showed that TOPSIS produces stable rankings close to the Pareto-optimal ordering across a range of problem structures, supporting its use in prioritisation tasks under uncertainty.

TOPSIS constructs a normalised weighted decision matrix, identifies ideal best and worst values per criterion, and ranks alternatives by their closeness coefficient, the ratio of the distance to the ideal worst to the sum of both distances [@albarakManagingTechnicalDebt2022]. A well-known limitation is rank reversal: adding or removing alternatives can shift the rankings of existing options [@garcia-cascalesRankReversalTOPSIS2012]. @yangIngeniousSolutionRank2020 proposed a normalisation adjustment that mitigates this by defining reference points independently of the current alternative set, which is particularly relevant for enterprise portfolios where services are regularly added or decommissioned.

Three alternative MCDM methods warrant comparison. VIKOR identifies compromise solutions between conflicting criteria rather than producing a global ranking, making it less appropriate when the output required is a ranked remediation list [@shekhovtsovComparativeCaseStudy2020]. AHP derives weights through pairwise comparisons but does not natively produce a full portfolio ranking, and the comparison process grows burdensome as the number of criteria increases [@ishakAnalysisFuzzyAHPTOPSIS2020]. A weighted scoring matrix is simpler still but assumes criteria are measured on comparable scales, which does not hold when mixing continuous operational metrics with categorical compliance indicators. TOPSIS integrates normalisation, weighting and ranking into a single procedure, making it the most efficient choice for generating a ranked service portfolio. Where expert-derived weights are needed, AHP pairwise comparisons can serve as an upstream step before feeding the results into TOPSIS, a combination demonstrated by @ishakAnalysisFuzzyAHPTOPSIS2020 in a regulated operational context.

@albarakManagingTechnicalDebt2022 provides the most directly analogous precedent, combining TOPSIS with portfolio theory to prioritise technical debt and showing that it surfaced high-priority items likely to be deferred under effort-only approaches. TOPSIS has also been applied in other risk-intensive, regulated contexts [@zytoonDecisionSupportModel2020; @khanIntegratedMethodologyRanking2022], providing methodological precedents for its use here. None of these studies, however, operates on live service management data in a large enterprise setting, which is a defining characteristic of the present research.

## Transferability Critique and Ethical Dimensions

Although the preceding sections provide strong motivation for the proposed framework, none of the existing applications reviewed operates on live operational records in large enterprise settings. Most rely on researcher-defined scenarios, synthetic datasets or controlled development artefacts. The proposed research differs by applying TOPSIS to operational records such as incident logs, ITSM change data and vulnerability scan outputs, which are known to have quality issues. Incident classifications can vary between teams. Recorded change failure rates can be influenced by documentation practices as much as by technical quality. Vulnerability counts depend on scanner coverage and configuration. These data limitations must be explicitly acknowledged. The sensitivity of rankings to input variation must be studied before outputs can be interpreted with confidence [@grecoMethodologicalFrameworkComposite2019].

Using operational service management data as a basis for technical debt prioritisation also raises important ethical questions. Such records are generated and owned by operations teams, and development teams may not have consented to their use in performance evaluations. A framework that ranks services based on operational indicators may influence budgets, staffing and reputational assessments in regulated institutions. These considerations are addressed in full in the methodology chapter.

## Synthesis: Converging Evidence and the Research Gap

The four thematic sections together define the conceptual, methodological, contextual and technical landscape within which this research is situated.

### Cross-Pillar Convergence

The following diagram illustrates the logical pipeline of the proposed framework, from the collection of operational indicators through to a ranked prioritisation output.
```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-framework
%%| fig-cap: "Proposed framework pipeline"
%%| fig-width: 6

flowchart LR
    A[Operational Indicators] --> B[Normalisation & Weighting] --> C[TOPSIS Scoring] --> D[Ranked Prioritisation]
```

Four converging findings from the preceding sections underpin this pipeline and define its scope. First, the concept of technical debt has expanded to encompass all layers of the enterprise IT stack, but measurement practice has not kept pace. Dominant tools still focus on code-level debt, leaving infrastructure lifecycle debt, platform obsolescence and vulnerability debt largely unmeasured [@kruchtenTechnicalDebtMetaphor2012; @yangTaxonomyTechnicalDebt2019; @biazottoTechnicalDebtManagement2024].

Second, technical debt prioritisation is both empirically and theoretically a multi-criteria problem. To the best of current literature, no widely accepted quantitative framework currently exists that integrates cost, value, risk and operational impact into a single, auditable ranking [@lenarduzziSystematicLiteratureReview2021]. Practitioner studies confirm that developers and managers routinely consider multiple factors when making repayment decisions [@alfayezHowSonarQubeidentifiedTechnical2023; @pinaTechnicalDebtPrioritization2022].

Third, the consequences of unmanaged technical debt are both measurable and substantial. Targeted repayment of architectural debt can dramatically reduce incidents [@detoledoReducingIncidentsMicroservices2021]. The financial harm of technical debt compounds progressively over a system's lifecycle, with median-debt firms seeing their system's net value turn negative within five years of adoption [@bankerTechnicalDebtFirm2020]. Repeated customisation of COTS systems can drive organisations into debt-constrained states [@rinta-kahilaGettingTrappedTechnical2023]. Poor code quality reduces throughput and increases defects [@tornhillCodeRedBusiness2022]. Delayed patching measurably increases vulnerability to exploitation [@tizioSoftwareUpdatesStrategies2023]. Together, these studies show that technical debt produces quantifiable, progressive operational harm.

Fourth, DORA metrics and related operational indicators offer a plausible bridge between technical debt and observable service-level performance [@forsgrenAccelerate2018]. The DORA-to-debt inference is not yet empirically established as a general relationship, and the present framework advances it explicitly as a core research hypothesis rather than a given premise. Positioning it as such, and committing to test it in the evaluation phase, is itself a theoretical contribution of this research.

### Cross-Cutting Limitations

Several limitations qualify these conclusions. The empirical base for financial IT and trading systems remains relatively thin. Studies at Credit Suisse, at BankAlpha and at ING each provide valuable insights but are restricted to a single organisation [@hakiDigitalNudgingTechnical2023; @rollandExploringTensionsManagement2021; @kulaReleasingFastSlow2019]. This scarcity of empirical evidence from trading IT environments specifically represents a gap that the present research is positioned to address, contributing an operational dataset from a regulated financial environment that is currently absent from the technical debt literature.

Existing applications of TOPSIS to technical debt rely on controlled datasets rather than live operational records [@albarakManagingTechnicalDebt2022; @khanIntegratedMethodologyRanking2022]. The comparative work of @shekhovtsovComparativeCaseStudy2020 shows that method choice matters less than weight specification when weights are uncertain, which underscores the importance of transparent and justified weight determination over method selection per se.

Behavioural studies emphasise that management intent, incentives and communication patterns often exert a stronger influence on remediation outcomes than technical tools [@wieseITManagersPerspective2023; @beskerTechnicalDebtEmpirical2020; @hakiDigitalNudgingTechnical2023]. Technical frameworks can support but not replace governance and leadership.

### The Research Gap

To the best of current literature, no framework integrates service-level operational metrics, multi-criteria prioritisation and the specific constraints of financial trading IT into a single, auditable technical debt ranking instrument. Code-centric tools are structurally inapplicable to vendor-hosted and regulated platforms. Single-criterion models conflict with both the empirical evidence on practitioner behaviour and the multidimensional nature of operational risk in trading environments. The proposed framework addresses this gap directly. Its evaluation will follow the approach established in adjacent quantitative work, assessing ranking stability across weighting schemes and validating indicator scores against historical operational records [@grecoMethodologicalFrameworkComposite2019; @albarakManagingTechnicalDebt2022].


\clearpage
# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
